name: Costa Rica
tagline: From the chiefdoms and stone spheres of the Diquis to a disarmed democracy of coffee, peace, and rainforest.
summary: Costa Rica occupies a narrow isthmus that long served as a bridge between the civilisations of Mesoamerica and the Andes, where chiefdom societies left the enigmatic stone spheres of the Diquis. Reached by Columbus in 1502 and named the Rich Coast, it became one of Spain's poorest and most isolated colonies, a province of subsistence smallholders within the Captaincy General of Guatemala. Independence came on 15 September 1821 as part of Central America, and after a spell in a regional federation Costa Rica emerged as a separate republic, its society built on coffee and bananas. A brief civil war in 1948 led to the abolition of the army in 1949, since when the country has become renowned for stable democracy, social welfare, and the conservation of its extraordinary biodiversity.

founding.label: Independence from Spain
founding.detail: On 15 September 1821, following the news that Mexico had broken with Spain, the Captaincy General of Guatemala proclaimed the independence of Central America, and Costa Rica with it. The remote province first joined the short-lived Mexican Empire, then in 1823 helped form the United Provinces of Central America. Disenchanted with the strife among the federation's other states, Costa Rica severed its ties in 1838, and in 1848 it declared itself a fully sovereign republic.

quickFacts.label: Earliest heritage
quickFacts.value: Stone spheres of the Diquis, chiefdom societies c. 500-1500 CE

quickFacts.label: European contact
quickFacts.value: Columbus reached the coast in 1502, naming it the Rich Coast

quickFacts.label: Separate republic
quickFacts.value: Withdrew from the federation 1838; republic declared 1848

eras.title: Chiefdoms and the Stone Spheres
eras.standfirst: On the isthmus between Mesoamerica and the Andes, indigenous peoples built ranked chiefdoms and carved the perfect stone spheres of the Diquis, among the most enigmatic monuments of the ancient Americas.
body: Human settlement in what is now Costa Rica reaches back thousands of years. By the time of European contact the territory lay at a cultural crossroads, a bridge across which goods, peoples, and ideas passed between the Mesoamerican civilisations to the north and the chiefdoms of Colombia and the Andes to the south. Dozens of small indigenous groups inhabited the land, organised not into great empires but into ranked societies of village and clan. They worked gold and jade, traded across long distances, and developed distinctive ceramic and lapidary traditions shaped by their position between two great cultural spheres.

In the low-lying deltas of the Diquis region in the country's south-east, chiefdom societies flourishing between roughly 500 and 1500 CE produced one of the most remarkable archaeological legacies of the pre-Columbian Americas: an assemblage of more than three hundred stone spheres. Ranging from a few centimetres to about 2.57 metres across and ground to near-perfect roundness without metal tools, the spheres were set in deliberate arrangements within settlements of mounds, paved areas, and burial grounds. Their precise meaning, use, and method of production remain only partly understood.

The settlement sites of Finca 6, Batambal, El Silencio, and Grijalba-2 preserve this culture in situ, the spheres standing where their makers placed them. In 2014 UNESCO inscribed the Precolumbian Chiefdom Settlements with Stone Spheres of the Diquis on its World Heritage List, the first cultural site in Costa Rica to receive the distinction. The inscription recognised the spheres as unique testimony to the complex social, economic, and political systems of these chiefdoms, a monumental achievement of a society that left no written record but a legacy carved in stone.

events.title: Diquis chiefdoms and the stone spheres
events.summary: Chiefdom societies in the Diquis delta carve more than 300 near-perfect stone spheres and build settlements of mounds and paved precincts.

events.title: UNESCO World Heritage inscription
events.summary: The Diquis settlements with their stone spheres become Costa Rica's first cultural World Heritage Site, recognised as unique evidence of pre-Columbian chiefdom societies.

pullquote.text: A collection of stone spheres, between 0.7 m and 2.57 m in diameter, whose meaning, use and production remain largely a mystery.
pullquote.attribution: UNESCO World Heritage Centre, on the Diquis spheres

eras.title: The Rich Coast: A Poor Colony
eras.standfirst: Named the Rich Coast by Columbus but yielding little gold, Costa Rica became one of Spain's poorest and most isolated provinces, a society of subsistence smallholders within the Captaincy General of Guatemala.
body: Christopher Columbus reached the Caribbean coast in 1502, during his fourth voyage, at a time when the land was home to numerous small indigenous tribes. Gifts of gold brought by the indigenous people gave rise to the name Costa Rica, the Rich Coast, in expectation of mineral wealth. That promise proved hollow. Conquest and disease devastated the indigenous population, yet the Spaniards found no rich mines, and permanent settlement came only slowly. The town of Cartago, founded in 1563 in the temperate Central Valley, became the colonial capital and remained so until 1823.

Theoretically governed by the captain general of Guatemala and the bishop of Leon in Nicaragua, Costa Rica was in practice ignored by both. The absence of precious metals meant few taxes were collected from the ticos, as Costa Ricans are known, and the crown invested little in roads or institutions. Distant, sparsely populated, and lacking a large subjugated indigenous labour force, the province developed differently from its neighbours. Settlers eked out a subsistence economy based on crops such as cacao and tobacco, working their own land rather than depending on great estates and bound labour.

From this poverty and isolation grew a society of comparatively egalitarian smallholders, a heritage later mythologised as the root of Costa Rican democracy. The province was peripheral to the wealth and conflicts of the empire, and its small population, concentrated in the highland valley, lived modestly and apart. When the structure of Spanish rule in Central America finally gave way in the early nineteenth century, Costa Rica entered independence as a quiet, agrarian backwater on the southern edge of the isthmus.

events.title: Columbus reaches the Rich Coast
events.summary: On his fourth voyage Columbus lands on the Caribbean coast; gifts of gold inspire the name Costa Rica, the Rich Coast.

events.title: Founding of Cartago
events.summary: The Spanish establish Cartago in the Central Valley as the colonial capital, which it remains until 1823.

figures.name: Juan Vasquez de Coronado
figures.blurb: Conquistador credited with the pacification and early settlement of Costa Rica, who founded the colony's first stable towns in the Central Valley and was named its governor before drowning at sea.

pullquote.text: The absence of precious metals meant the collection of few taxes from the ticos; consequently Spain provided little help in developing the infrastructure of the province.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedia Britannica, History of Costa Rica

eras.title: Independence and a Coffee Republic
eras.standfirst: Independence in 1821, membership of a fractious Central American federation, and the rise of coffee transformed an isolated province into a sovereign republic with a precocious tradition of elections.
body: When Central America proclaimed its separation from Spain on 15 September 1821, Costa Rica was drawn into events largely beyond its control. It joined the brief Mexican Empire, then in 1823 helped form the United Provinces of Central America, adopting the federal constitution of 1824. Juan Mora Fernandez, elected the nation's first head of state in 1824, gave the young polity a measure of order: he founded schools, established a judiciary and roads, and above all encouraged coffee cultivation by granting free land to those who would plant it. Disenchanted with the federation's chronic strife, Costa Rica withdrew in 1838.

Coffee made the republic. From the 1840s a constant stream of oxcarts carried beans from the Central Valley down to Pacific ports and on to Europe, and the wealth it generated created a powerful planter elite while financing the institutions of an independent state. In 1848 Costa Rica formally declared itself a sovereign republic. Its early politics, though dominated by a coffee oligarchy, developed a comparatively stable and orderly character, and by the close of the century the country had begun to hold elections widely regarded as the first free and honest contests in Central America.

Sovereignty was tested in the mid-1850s when the American filibuster William Walker seized power in Nicaragua and threatened the whole isthmus. Costa Rica joined the other Central American states in the National War of 1856-57 that drove Walker out. From the campaign emerged the country's most cherished patriotic legend, the young soldier Juan Santamaria, honoured ever since as a national hero. By the 1880s a new export, the banana, was taking root on the Caribbean lowlands, reshaping the economy once more.

events.title: Independence from Spain
events.summary: Central America, Costa Rica included, declares independence from Spain; the province soon joins the Mexican Empire and then a regional federation.

events.title: Juan Mora Fernandez, first head of state
events.summary: Costa Rica's first elected head of state founds schools and courts and promotes coffee planting through free land grants.

events.title: Withdrawal from the federation
events.summary: Disenchanted with the strife of the United Provinces of Central America, Costa Rica severs its federal ties and charts an independent course.

events.title: National War against William Walker
events.summary: Costa Rica joins Central America in defeating the American filibuster William Walker; the soldier Juan Santamaria becomes a national hero.

figures.name: Juan Mora Fernandez
figures.role: First head of state of Costa Rica
figures.blurb: Teacher who served as Costa Rica's first elected head of state from 1824, founding schools and a judiciary and encouraging the coffee planting that would underwrite the nation's prosperity.

figures.name: Juan Santamaria
figures.blurb: Costa Rican soldier celebrated for his part in the defence against William Walker's filibusters in 1856. He is the country's foremost patriotic symbol, his name borne by its principal airport and a national museum.

pullquote.text: From the 1840s a constant stream of oxcarts carried coffee from the Valle Central to Pacific ports and ships bound for Europe.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedia Britannica, on the coffee economy

eras.title: Coffee, Bananas, and a Maturing Democracy
eras.standfirst: Banana enclaves and a deepening coffee trade bound Costa Rica into the world economy, while an expanding electorate and a tradition of orderly succession built one of Latin America's most stable polities, until the crisis of 1948.
body: The banana transformed the Caribbean lowlands. From 1872 the American entrepreneur Minor C. Keith built railways and planted bananas to serve the line, and in 1899 his interests merged into the United Fruit Company, which became a dominant economic and political force across Central America. Costa Rica thus came to rest on two great exports: highland coffee, grown by a settled landowning class, and lowland bananas, cultivated on foreign-owned plantations worked largely by Afro-Caribbean and immigrant labour. The contrast shaped the country's society, geography, and politics for generations.

Politically, Costa Rica matured more peacefully than its neighbours. In 1890 it held what is widely considered the first free and honest election in Central America, inaugurating a tradition of orderly, competitive succession unusual in the region. The franchise widened, public education and a literate citizenry expanded, and despite occasional coups and disputed votes the rule of law and civilian government took firmer root than almost anywhere else in the isthmus. By the early twentieth century the country had earned a reputation for relative civility and stability.

That reputation was shaken in the 1940s. Reformist governments under Rafael Angel Calderon Guardia introduced a labour code and social-security system, but amid economic strain and bitter partisanship the politics of the decade grew polarised. The crisis came to a head over the presidential election of 1948: when Calderon's faction, having lost, sought to annul the result and bar the victor Otilio Ulate from office, the country was plunged into armed conflict.

events.title: Minor Keith and the banana railways
events.summary: Minor C. Keith begins building railways and planting bananas, ventures that in 1899 merge into the United Fruit Company and reshape the Caribbean coast.

events.title: First free election in Central America
events.summary: Costa Rica holds what is regarded as the first free and honest election in Central America, beginning a long democratic tradition.

events.title: Disputed election sparks civil war
events.summary: The annulment of the 1948 election to bar president-elect Otilio Ulate triggers a brief but bloody civil war.

figures.name: Minor C. Keith
figures.blurb: Builder of Costa Rica's railways and founder of its banana trade, whose enterprises coalesced into the United Fruit Company, the dominant commercial power of Central America for half a century.

eras.title: Civil War and the Abolition of the Army
eras.standfirst: A two-month civil war led by Jose Figueres Ferrer ended in a new constitution that, in 1949, abolished the standing army, a defining choice that set the course of modern Costa Rica.
body: When the Legislative Assembly annulled the 1948 election and moved to reinstall Rafael Angel Calderon Guardia, Jose Figueres Ferrer, who had stockpiled arms on his farm near Cartago, rose in revolt in support of the president-elect Otilio Ulate. His forces, which would later become the National Liberation Party, launched a successful rebellion in a brief civil war that cost roughly two thousand lives, the bloodiest episode in twentieth-century Costa Rican history. Calderon's government, despite backing from the Nicaraguan strongman Anastasio Somoza, capitulated within two months.

Figueres headed a governing junta for some eighteen months and used the moment to refound the republic. The constitution his regime promulgated in 1949 made the country's most consequential and enduring decision: it prohibited the establishment or maintenance of a standing army. The same charter extended suffrage to women, strengthened the independent electoral tribunal, abolished institutionalised racism, nationalised the banking system, and created powerful autonomous state agencies to drive development and welfare.

Having reshaped the state, Figueres handed power to the rightful victor, Otilio Ulate, in 1949, honouring the election his rebellion had defended. Figueres himself would return as elected president, and the National Liberation Party he founded became a pillar of the new order. The abolition of the army, far from leaving Costa Rica defenceless, freed resources for education, health, and social spending, and became the foundation of the country's later identity as a stable, demilitarised democracy.

events.title: Figueres leads the civil war
events.summary: Jose Figueres Ferrer leads an armed uprising in support of president-elect Ulate; the two-month war kills about 2,000 people before the government surrenders.

events.title: Constitution abolishes the army
events.summary: Figueres's junta promulgates a new constitution that bans a standing army, grants women the vote, and nationalises the banks, then hands power to Ulate.

figures.name: Jose Figueres Ferrer
figures.blurb: Leader of the 1948 uprising and head of the junta that abolished the army in 1949. Founder of the National Liberation Party and three times president, he is the architect of Costa Rica's modern social democracy.

figures.name: Otilio Ulate Blanco
figures.blurb: Newspaper publisher whose contested 1948 victory triggered the civil war fought on his behalf. He assumed the presidency in 1949 and oversaw the consolidation of the new constitutional order.

pullquote.text: A new constitution, promulgated in 1949, prohibited the establishment or maintenance of an army.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedia Britannica, on Jose Figueres Ferrer

eras.title: Peace, Welfare, and the Green Republic
eras.standfirst: Without an army, Costa Rica built one of Latin America's most stable democracies and generous welfare states, won a Nobel Peace Prize, and made the conservation of its biodiversity central to its identity and economy.
body: In the decades after 1949 Costa Rica became a byword for democratic stability. Power alternated peacefully and regularly between parties, the social-security and education systems expanded, and the country avoided the dictatorships and civil wars that scarred its neighbours through the Cold War. Its standing as a peace-broker reached its height in the 1980s, when President Oscar Arias Sanchez devised a regional peace plan for the wars then convulsing Central America. Arias and the other regional leaders signed it in August 1987, and that year he was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize.

From the 1970s conservation became a national mission. A national park system created in 1970 grew to protect about a quarter of the country's territory, sheltering tropical rainforest, cloud forest, and dry forest, and safeguarding a biodiversity that includes a remarkable share of the world's known species. Ecotourism flourished around reserves such as Monteverde and Corcovado, and the protection of forests became, for many landowners, more rewarding than clearing them. The once agrarian economy shifted toward services, technology, and tourism, with the green republic as its brand.

Into the twenty-first century Costa Rica has sustained its reputation as one of the most stable, peaceful, and democratic nations of the Americas, governed without a standing army for more than seventy years. It contends with the familiar pressures of inequality, public debt, and regional migration, yet remains a small country of outsized influence, holding together the threads of its history: the egalitarian smallholder past, the coffee and banana economies, the disarmed democracy of 1949, and the modern ethos of peace and environmental stewardship.

events.title: National park system created
events.summary: Costa Rica establishes a national park system that grows to protect roughly a quarter of the country, anchoring its conservation and ecotourism economy.

events.title: Oscar Arias wins the Nobel Peace Prize
events.summary: President Oscar Arias Sanchez is awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for the Central American peace plan signed by the region's leaders in August 1987.

events.title: Diquis spheres named World Heritage
events.summary: UNESCO inscribes the Diquis chiefdom settlements and their stone spheres as Costa Rica's first cultural World Heritage Site.

figures.name: Oscar Arias Sanchez
figures.blurb: President in 1986-90 and 2006-10 whose Central American peace plan earned him the 1987 Nobel Peace Prize, embodying Costa Rica's identity as a demilitarised broker of peace.

pullquote.text: Of all the Central American countries, Costa Rica is generally regarded as having the most stable and most democratic government.

figures.name: Jose Figueres Ferrer
figures.blurb: Leader of the 1948 civil war and head of the junta that abolished Costa Rica's army in 1949, founder of the National Liberation Party, and architect of the modern social-democratic state.

figures.name: Juan Mora Fernandez
figures.blurb: Costa Rica's first elected head of state from 1824, who built early institutions and promoted the coffee economy that underpinned the republic.

figures.name: Oscar Arias Sanchez
figures.blurb: Two-term president whose Central American peace plan won the 1987 Nobel Peace Prize, symbolising Costa Rica's vocation for peace.
